sunday.themeNote: This Sunday's readings paint a picture of a God who comes not with force or fanfare, but with gentleness and peace. From Zechariah's humble king riding on a donkey to Jesus inviting the tired and burdened to find rest in him, we see that God's way is one of mercy, not might. The Holy Spirit, Paul reminds us, is the living power that makes this new, lighter way of life possible.

readings.label: First Reading
readings.fullText:
Thus says the LORD:
Rejoice heartily, O daughter Zion,
shout for joy, O daughter Jerusalem!
See, your king shall come to you;
a just savior is he,
meek, and riding on an ass,
on a colt, the foal of an ass.
He shall banish the chariot from Ephraim,
and the horse from Jerusalem;
the warrior's bow shall be banished,
and he shall proclaim peace to the nations.
His dominion shall be from sea to sea,
and from the River to the ends of the earth.
readings.summary: Written hundreds of years before Jesus was born, this passage from the prophet Zechariah describes a coming king unlike any the world had ever seen. Instead of riding a warhorse and conquering by force, this king arrives humbly on a donkey and brings peace to all nations. When Jesus entered Jerusalem on Palm Sunday, the people recognized this prophecy being fulfilled before their eyes. God's kingdom, it turns out, looks very different from the kingdoms of this world.

readings.label: Responsorial Psalm
readings.fullText:
R. (cf. 1) I will praise your name for ever, my king and my God.
or:
R. Alleluia.
I will extol you, O my God and King,
and I will bless your name forever and ever.
Every day will I bless you,
and I will praise your name forever and ever.
R. I will praise your name for ever, my king and my God.
or:
R. Alleluia.
The LORD is gracious and merciful,
slow to anger and of great kindness.
The LORD is good to all
and compassionate toward all his works.
R. I will praise your name for ever, my king and my God.
or:
R. Alleluia.
Let all your works give you thanks, O LORD,
and let your faithful ones bless you.
Let them discourse of the glory of your kingdom
and speak of your might.
R. I will praise your name for ever, my king and my God.
or:
R.  Alleluia.
The LORD is faithful in all his words
and holy in all his works.
The LORD lifts up all who are falling
and raises up all who are bowed down.
R. I will praise your name for ever, my king and my God.
or:
R. Alleluia.
readings.summary: Psalm 145 is a joyful song of praise to God the King — not a distant or demanding ruler, but one who is gracious, merciful, and full of kindness. The psalm reminds us that God is good to everyone and lifts up those who are struggling or feel beaten down by life. As we sing or recite this psalm at Mass, we join our voices to centuries of believers who found hope in a God who truly cares. It's an invitation to make praise a daily habit, not just a Sunday one.

readings.label: Second Reading
readings.fullText:
Brothers and sisters:
You are not in the flesh;
on the contrary, you are in the spirit,
if only the Spirit of God dwells in you.
Whoever does not have the Spirit of Christ does not belong to him.
If the Spirit of the one who raised Jesus from the dead dwells in you,
the one who raised Christ from the dead
will give life to your mortal bodies also,
through his Spirit that dwells in you.
Consequently, brothers and sisters,
we are not debtors to the flesh,
to live according to the flesh.
For if you live according to the flesh, you will die,
but if by the Spirit you put to death the deeds of the body,
you will live.
readings.summary: Paul is writing to early Christians in Rome, reminding them that something has genuinely changed in them since they came to faith. The Holy Spirit — God's own presence — now lives within them, and that changes everything about how they live. Rather than being driven by selfish impulses and habits that lead nowhere good, they are now empowered by the same Spirit who raised Jesus from the dead. This isn't about following rules by willpower alone; it's about a new life made possible from the inside out.

readings.label: Gospel
readings.fullText:
At that time Jesus exclaimed:
"I give praise to you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth,
for although you have hidden these things
from the wise and the learned
you have revealed them to little ones.
Yes, Father, such has been your gracious will.
All things have been handed over to me by my Father.
No one knows the Son except the Father,
and no one knows the Father except the Son
and anyone to whom the Son wishes to reveal him."

"Come to me, all you who labor and are burdened,
and I will give you rest.
Take my yoke upon you and learn from me,
for I am meek and humble of heart;
and you will find rest for yourselves.
For my yoke is easy, and my burden light."
readings.summary: Jesus begins by thanking his Father for something surprising: that the deepest truths about God are often received not by the cleverest people, but by those who approach with open, humble hearts. Then comes one of the most beloved invitations in all of Scripture — come to me if you are tired and weighed down, and I will give you rest. Jesus describes himself as meek and humble, and says that following him is not an added burden but actually a relief. Whatever heavy load you've been carrying — guilt, worry, loneliness — he is offering to share it with you.
